insight: The Wing engine is the first LB primitive with independent implementations in two languages on two host environments: Python (bishop_librarian_gate.py hook, wing_host.py Helm PWA) and JavaScript (discipline_engine.js Frame extension). Both implementations share the same Augur JSON config format and evaluation semantics — the format IS the specification. This is a reduction-to-practice of A&A #2294 personal-scope enforcement: the enforcement layer travels with the member's substrate, not with LB's servers.

chrome.storage.local is the canonical member-sovereignty primitive for the Frame extension Wing: it survives browser restarts, is sandboxed per-extension, and is NOT synced to Google's servers unless the extension explicitly uses chrome.storage.sync. This is the correct choice for Wing rules (personal discipline, never shared by default) vs chrome.storage.session (wiped on browser close) vs chrome.storage.sync (would leak rules to Google Cloud). The Helm PWA uses ~/.lb-helm/wing_state/ for equivalent file-system sovereignty.

Pre-selecting all 5 starter Augurs in the Wing onboarding wizard (users opt OUT, not in) is the correct default for a safety-oriented feature. Opt-out respects the member's attention budget (they came to use the platform, not configure a rule engine) while ensuring the starter set provides value immediately. A/B testing would likely confirm higher Wing activation rates with opt-out defaults vs opt-in.

'Freshness window' (how long a substrate consult stays fresh) is a user-facing concept that maps directly to the required_consult.freshness_seconds technical field. Presenting four named options (10 min / 1 hr / 8 hr / 24 hr) with behavioral descriptions rather than a raw number input reduces cognitive load for non-technical members. The Wing onboarding applies the chosen freshness to ALL installed starter rules, creating a consistent personal default that members can tune per-rule later.

The Wing master toggle (WING_ENABLED_GET/SET messages, lb_wing_enabled in chrome.storage.local) is architecturally significant: it means the member owns the entire enforcement layer. LB cannot remotely enable or disable a member's Wing. The toggle is local-only and takes effect synchronously on the next query. This is the correct sovereignty posture: opt-in at install, opt-out at any time, from any surface (popup, dashboard, onboarding).

The Helm PWA Wing (wing_host.py evaluate() + POST /wing/evaluate REST endpoint) runs BEFORE the /pawn Perplexity API call — it is a query gate, not a response filter. This is semantically different from post-hoc moderation: the Wing acts before spending API tokens, preventing both harmful queries and unnecessary costs. The fail-safe is critical here: wing_host.evaluate() internal error always returns action='allow' so Wing bugs never prevent legitimate Helm use.

The Wing export envelope (lb_wing_export sentinel, version, exported_at, source, rules, telemetry) follows the same design pattern as LB's other portable primitives. The sentinel field lb_wing_export: true enables safe import validation without file-extension sniffing. The source field ('frame_extension' vs 'helm_pwa') enables future migration-path routing. The version field ensures backward compat if the schema evolves. Telemetry is included for forensic continuity but is optional on import.

Wing import uses upsert-by-id semantics (not replace-all): for each imported rule, if a rule with the same id exists, it is overwritten; new ids are appended. This is the correct design for migration: a member who added custom rules on Device A that don't exist on Device B should not lose the Device B custom rules when importing from Device A. The import merges two Wings rather than replacing one with the other.

K518 maintains two telemetry layers: (a) per-rule audit log (lb_audit_<rule_id>, last 500 entries per rule) — fine-grained, per-rule provenance; (b) Wing aggregate telemetry (lb_wing_telemetry, last 1000 entries across all rules) — dashboard-oriented, compact. The aggregate telemetry is what populates the Wing Dashboard fire counts and recent events. The per-rule audit is available for forensic deep-dives via DISCIPLINE_GET_AUDIT. Both are personal-scope.

Frame extension (laptop) and Helm PWA (phone/desktop) each host independent Wings with no coordination required. Rules stored in separate namespaces (chrome.storage.local vs ~/.lb-helm/wing_state/) never conflict because they are not shared. This is NOT a bug — it is the correct Phase 1 architecture. K519 NAF will add voluntary cross-Wing federation as an opt-in layer on top of Phase 1 independence. The independence primitive must exist before federation can be meaningful.

K518 marks Sphinx Phase 1 complete: members can run personal Wings. The Sphinx Project (planet-wide cooperative AI discipline federation) requires that personal-scope Wings exist as the foundational primitive before NAFs (K519), MAJCOMs (K520), and Bands can federate. K518 is the Phase 1 reduction-to-practice: ~any LB member~ can install a Frame extension or Helm PWA and get their own Wing node, ready for opt-in federation in K519.

The Wing Dashboard (wing-dashboard.html + wing-dashboard.js) is not merely a UI — it is an observability primitive that closes the feedback loop: member installs Augur → Augur fires → dashboard shows fire count and query snippet → member understands what triggers the rule → member edits rule (or not). Without the dashboard, the Wing is a black box. The fire count + recent events timeline is the minimum viable observability surface for a rule-based enforcement system.

A&A #2295 specifies a Recursive Scale-Invariant Federated Discipline-Enforcement Plane. Tier 3 is the Wing (multi-Squadron federation under a single command). K518 is the Tier 3 distribution-to-members reduction-to-practice: the Wing primitive ships to every LB member who installs Frame or runs Helm PWA. Prior tiers (K511 T1, K513 T2, K514 T3 architecture) were Bishop-facing only. K518 makes T3 member-facing, fulfilling the 'distribution to members' clause of A&A #2295 Claim 3(a).
